era_context: Late Middle Bronze Age. Egypt's 13th Dynasty was in decline, with Hyksos infiltration ongoing. The Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating in Anatolia. Mesopotamia was characterized by competing city-states, with Babylon still a secondary power under Sin-muballit. The broader era was transitional, with older city-state systems giving way to larger territorial kingdoms.

events.title: Continued fragmentation of Egyptian 13th Dynasty
events.description: The Egyptian 13th Dynasty continued its fragmentation, with weak pharaohs and rapid succession. Asiatic groups (Hyksos) were increasingly settling in the Delta region, establishing their own power bases and eventually founding the 15th Dynasty. This period marks the deepening of the Second Intermediate Period, characterized by political fragmentation and foreign settlement.
key_figures: Various 13th Dynasty pharaohs (names and order disputed)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Delta sites (Tell el-Dab'a, Avaris)

events.certainty_note: The 13th Dynasty's chronology is uncertain by ±15–20 years. The year -1794 is assigned by convention as part of the documented fragmentation period, but precise dating is not possible.

events.title: Hittite Old Kingdom consolidation in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom under Hantili I and his successors continued consolidating power in central Anatolia. This period saw the formalization of Hittite administrative structures, the establishment of Hattusa as the capital, and the beginning of territorial expansion. The Hittites were establishing themselves as a major power in Anatolia, challenging Assyrian and other regional powers.
key_figures: Hantili I (Hittite king) or his successor

sources.name: Hittite King List and royal inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The Hittite Old Kingdom's chronology is uncertain by ±20 years. The year -1794 is assigned by convention as part of the consolidation period.

disconfirming_evidence: The year -1794 has no particular significance in the historical record; it is assigned by convention as part of broader periods of fragmentation and consolidation. The Egyptian 13th Dynasty's chronology is one of the most disputed topics in Egyptology, with competing chronologies differing by over 100 years. The Hittite Old Kingdom's dating is also uncertain. The significance of this year as a turning point is entirely modern historiographic convention, not based on contemporary evidence.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1794 is extremely sparse. For Egypt, sources consist of king lists compiled centuries later and archaeological evidence from Delta sites. For Anatolia, sources consist of later Hittite king lists and archaeological evidence. There are no contemporary written sources that document events of this specific year. The surviving record is heavily biased toward Egypt and Anatolia; Mesopotamia, the Levant, and other regions are poorly documented. The broader context of this year is largely reconstructed from archaeological evidence and later historical compilations, both of which are subject to significant uncertainty.

graph_edges.note: Continued 13th Dynasty fragmentation eventually led to Hyksos rule in the 15th Dynasty
Hittite consolidation in this period led to the expansion of the Old Kingdom
